coredata.dc:title: A natural product-derived nanozyme regulator induced chemo-ferroptosis dual therapy in remodeling of the tumor immune microenvironment of hepatocellular carcinoma
coredata.dc:creator: Lai, Chun-Mei
Xu, Jia
Zhang, Bing-Chen
He, Shao-Hua
Shao, Jing-Wei
coredata.dc:description: Ferroptosis, an emerging cell death mode discovered in recent decades, is characterized by lipid peroxidation during cell death, usually manifested as iron accumulation. However, the forthright delivery of iron species will cause adverse detrimental effects such as anaphylactic reactions in routine tissues. So far, studies on cellular ferroptosis by employing bio-derived nanomaterials have rarely been acquired. Herein, we reported carbon dots (CDs)-based biocompatible enzymes with negligible toxicity from a representative hepatoprotective triterpenoid natural product ursolic acid (UA) with acknowledged antitumor activity. We observed that CDs presented distinct GSH oxidase-like characteristics and then contributed ferroptosis of carcinoma cells by interfering with the GPX4-catalyzed lipid repair system. After the CDs were assembled with UA, the obtained denoted as UCDs integrated multi-model therapy into one, resulting in enhanced therapeutic efficacy, reduced adverse effects, and optimized clinical outcomes. In vivo, the UCDs hysterically inhibited tumor growth in hepatoma H22-bearing mice with inconsequential toxicity. Exceptionally, UCDs enlisted a large number of tumor-infiltrating immune cells, including T cells, NK cells, and macrophages in H22 tumor-bearing mice, consequently transforming “cold” into “hot” tumors to activate systemic anti-tumor immune responses. Meanwhile, UCDs could also awfully suppress the H22-LUC tumor growth in orthotopic xenograft mouse models. Collectively, our research commends that the drug delivery systems based on natural-products-derived CDs can function as biocompatible enzymes for antitumor treatment and may facilitate the advancement of nanotechnology-based immunotherapeutics. It is extremely anticipated that such ferroptosis-like designing in nano-catalytic medicine would be favorable for future development in cancer-therapeutic regimens.

head.ce:title: A natural product-derived nanozyme regulator induced chemo-ferroptosis dual therapy in remodeling of the tumor immune microenvironment of hepatocellular carcinoma

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ferroptosis, an emerging cell death mode discovered in recent decades, is characterized by lipid peroxidation during cell death, usually manifested as iron accumulation. However, the forthright delivery of iron species will cause adverse detrimental effects such as anaphylactic reactions in routine tissues. So far, studies on cellular ferroptosis by employing bio-derived nanomaterials have rarely been acquired. Herein, we reported carbon dots (CDs)-based biocompatible enzymes with negligible toxicity from a representative hepatoprotective triterpenoid natural product ursolic acid (UA) with acknowledged antitumor activity. We observed that CDs presented distinct GSH oxidase-like characteristics and then contributed ferroptosis of carcinoma cells by interfering with the GPX4-catalyzed lipid repair system. After the CDs were assembled with UA, the obtained denoted as UCDs integrated multi-model therapy into one, resulting in enhanced therapeutic efficacy, reduced adverse effects, and optimized clinical outcomes. In vivo, the UCDs hysterically inhibited tumor growth in hepatoma H22-bearing mice with inconsequential toxicity. Exceptionally, UCDs enlisted a large number of tumor-infiltrating immune cells, including T cells, NK cells, and macrophages in H22 tumor-bearing mice, consequently transforming “cold” into “hot” tumors to activate systemic anti-tumor immune responses. Meanwhile, UCDs could also awfully suppress the H22-LUC tumor growth in orthotopic xenograft mouse models. Collectively, our research commends that the drug delivery systems based on natural-products-derived CDs can function as biocompatible enzymes for antitumor treatment and may facilitate the advancement of nanotechnology-based immunotherapeutics. It is extremely anticipated that such ferroptosis-like designing in nano-catalytic medicine would be favorable for future development in cancer-therapeutic regimens.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a recognized nanomaterial, nanozymes manifested inherent enzyme-like characteristics and were considered charming for drug targeting, diagnosis, and sensing [1]. Nevertheless, the promotion of conventional nanozymes is hindered by cumbersome synthesis processes, extravagant manufacturing costs, and unsatisfied biocompatibility. In particular, the extensively reported metal matrix (Au, Mn, and Ag) nanozymes are associated with a series of health anxious in clinical medical transformation [2]. Thus, it is imperative to develop nanozymes with creative safe materials, principally the ordinary foods obtainable around the world, which hold extraordinary potential in future biomedical fields.

There is growing interest in the health benefits associated with the consumption of fruits and vegetables, especially for the prevention of cancer or other diseases. Clinical trials suggest that these health benefits are strongly associated with phytochemicals found in fruits and vegetables. Ursolic acid (UA) is one of the naturally synthesized pentacyclic triterpenoids, widely distributed in different fruits and vegetables, it has the advantages of low toxicity and high efficacy compared with conventional chemotherapy agents, which has attracted increasing interest in scientists over the past few decades. Most importantly, our previous studies have shown that UA and its derivatives exhibited anticancer activity, anti-inflammatory effects, and induction of apoptosis in several human cancer cells [3,4]. Carbon dots (CDs) are amorphous fluorescence carbon nanomaterials sized in 2–10 nm [5]. Currently, natural-derived CDs have attracted broad interest in their outstanding features, including nontoxicity, biological safety, and eco-friendliness [5,6], making them ideal candidates for clinical practice in disease diagnosis and treatment. Particularly, the presence of diversified functional groups on the surface of CDs provides favorable biological capacities by simulating various enzyme catalytic activity sites. Mounting evidence has emerged demonstrating that CDs-based nanozyme simulate the structure and function of natural enzymes, especially for GSH oxidase or superoxide dismutase, thereby inducing a “storm” of active substances, amplifying the damage caused by reactive oxygen species (ROS)-based oxidative stress in biological systems to enhance therapeutic efficacy.

As a critical oxidative stress-dependent cell death, ferroptosis has attracted broad attention [7]. Mechanistically, ferroptosis is primarily controlled by the glutathione (GSH) redox system and features unrestrained lipid peroxidation and membrane degradation [8]. GSH consumption results in the inactivation of glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4) with the resultant accumulation of ROS from lipid peroxidation and subsequent ferroptosis [9]. In particular, the cancer cells undergoing ferroptosis have been reported to be immunogenic, as affected cells release damage-related molecular patterns and alerts [10,11], which promote immune activation. Therefore, ferroptosis may be an effective anticancer treatment strategy. Furthermore, the hepatoma carcinoma cells were “cold” tumors with immunogenicity, which were not sensitive to immunity and were not effective in clinical immunotherapy. Thus, the development of naturally derived CDs nanozymes that can induce ferroptosis at the tumor sites and activate the tumor immune response is imminent. Our previous studies have confirmed that UA has a comprehensive and diversified immune function, which can remarkably activate innate immunity and significantly increase T cells frequencies and numbers in the spleen and lymph nodes [11,12]. Even more, UA could not only cause the tumor cell's death and provide tumor antigens but also activate natural immunity including IL-12, IFN-g, T cells, B cells, and NK cells, and enhance APC cell proliferation at the same time, to realize the further clearance of tumor by acquired immune cells. Chemotherapy is regarded as a vital tool for the treatment of cancer [13]. Chemoimmunotherapy, an emerging tumor therapeutic strategy that combines immunotherapy with chemotherapy medicines, has the benefit of combining two therapeutic mechanisms in a way that minimizes drug dosage and maximizes therapeutic effects [14]. In addition, nano-DDSs have been utilized to encapsulate chemotherapeutic drugs and displayed excellent features such as tumor microenvironment (TME) stimulation response, nano effect, and controllable release [15]. However, research on the utilization of nanozymes produced from natural products to trigger tumor cell ferroptosis and anti-tumor immunotherapy is rare, let alone the combination of CDs-based nanozymes and chemotherapeutic drugs.

Given the combination of chemotherapy and immunotherapy has elegantly addressed the issue of off-target and adverse effects, herein, through the assembly of UA NPs and CDs, UCDs with GSH oxidase-like activity that oxidizes reduced glutathione (GSH) to oxidized glutathione (GSSG) were facilely prepared (Scheme 1 ). Through such a simple approach assembly synthesis process, the UCDs can be endowed with multiple remarkable features: 1) As a result of the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, the diameter of UCDs was increased in comparison to CDs alone, adding to their enrichment at tumor tissues [16]; 2) CDs catalyzed reduced GSH into oxidized GSSG, exhibiting remarkable GSH oxidase-like activities; 3) CDs induced HepG2 cells ferroptosis via facilitating imbalanced redox reaction ascribed to the depleting of GSH and the inactivating of GPX4; 4) In vivo, the CDs greatly inhibited the tumor growth via noticeably enhancing ferroptosis-dependent immune cell infiltration to turn immunologically “cold” of liver into “hot” tumors; 5) UCDs integrated chemotherapy drugs and immunotherapy into one system, which can synergistically advance the anti-tumor effect compared with any single treatment method. Altogether, we conducted a proof-of-concept experiment to determine whether the “One-for-All” nano-system based on the natural substance UA might be used as an anti-cancer ferroptosis starting agent for the activation of the tumor immune microenvironment (TIME), emphasizing the encouraging value of CDs in clinical translational medicine.

Ursolic acid (UA) (98 %), sorafenib (SF) (98 %), Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (99.9 %), Disodium terephthalate (TA) (99.7 %) and 2,2′-azinobis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)-diammonium salt (99.8 %) were available from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS), Dichlorodihydro fluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), Dil, and Mito-tracker were acquired from meilunbio. The glutathione (GSH) assay kit was obtained from Aladdin Industries Inc. (Shanghai, China). The Calcein-AM/PI double stain kit was obtained from Yeasen Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Antibodies against NRF2, COX-2, GPX4, and GAPDH were obtained from Cell Signaling Technology (Beverly, MA, USA). MTT [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide] was purchased from Life Technologies GMbH (Darmstadt, Germany). HepG2 cells, H22 cells, and H22-LUC were available from the Type Culture Collection of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). H22-LUC with stable luciferase expression was obtained from Meisen CTCC Technology Co., Ltd. (Zhejiang, China). All reagents for cell culture were obtained from Life Technologies GMbH (Darmstadt, Germany) unless specifically emphasized.

The UA-derived CDs were fabricated with one-pot hydrothermal treatment. To be specific, 60 mg of UA powders were soaked in 9 mL of ultra-pure water and stirred for 5–10 min each. Subsequently, removed to a 25 mL autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene, heated at 200 °C for 6 h, and cooled to 25℃ before filtering with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to obtain a clear solution. Finally, the CDs solution was available by dialysis (1 kDa cutoff) for 24 h.

Simply put, UCDs were prepared by eddy current mixing of 100 μL UA NPs aqueous solution (4 mg/mL) synthesized concerning our previously reported [17] was mixed with CDs at different mass ratios (20:1, 10:1, 5:1, 1:1, 1:5, 1:10, and 1: 20) to prepare UCDs by vortex mixing. Moreover, the physicochemical properties of UCDs (such as particle size, potential, etc.) can be optimized by adjusting the parameters including solvent utilized and drug ratios.

H22 cells, HepG2 cells, and H22-LUC cells were cultivated in a 37℃ constant temperature incubator containing 5 % CO2. The ratio of the medium was as follows: 10 % FBS + 90 % serum-free medium (DMEM or 1640) + 1 % penicillin and streptomycin (100 μg/mL). Among them, H22 cells and H22-LUC cells were grown in RPIM-1640 containing 10 % FBS, while HepG2 cells were grown in DMEM medium containing 10 % FBS.

The morphology of nanoparticles was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of the Talos F200i model at an accelerated voltage of 200 kV. Freshly prepared specimens were placed on the formvar stabilized with carbon support film and dried off excess solvent after 10 min. Hydrodynamic sizes and zeta potentials of nanoparticles were measured by the Zetasizer Nano (Malvern, England) at room temperature.

The thickness of the CDs was scanned with AFM (Multimode 8, Brooke, Germany). Concisely, 10 μg/mL of the samples to be tested was added to the clean mica substrate surface for three minutes before the ultrasound, and the mica surface was dried by nitrogen blowing before AFM imaging, which was processed with NanoScope V.

The solution of CDs (10 mg/mL) and UCDs at equivalent concentrations of CDs was placed in an optical quartz cuvette. The fluorescence spectrum of the photosensitizer to be measured was carried out in a FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrophotometer (France).

The sample was dried in a vacuum at 40℃ for two min, and then mixed with KBr at a mass ratio of 1:40, subsequently the infrared spectra ranged from 4000 to 400 cm−1 and were measured on an FT-IR 5700 spectrometer. All data collected were processed by OMNIC 32.

XPS spectra were recorded using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB250, Thermo Scientific) under ultra-high vacuum conditions. The bending energy was collected by using 284.5 e V graphite carbon C1s signal as a reference. All detected were fitted by Avantage software.

The particle size and zeta potentials of all being-tested samples were assessed by the Zsizer Nano (Malvern, England). To improve the accuracy of the experiment, the viscosity and refractive index of water at 25 °C were used as reference values.

GSH oxidase-like activity was evaluated by employing the 5,5′-dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) as the substrate with diverse concentrations of GSH [18]. The yellow products of GSH and DTNB were recorded by UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2700, Japan) every 5 min at a wavelength of 422 nm for a total of 50 min. For kinetic evaluation, the assessment was conducted on 75 μL PBS solution which contained 50 μg/mL CDs, 50 μM DTNB, and a gradient concentration of GSH (0–––4 mM).
H22-LUC cells and HepG2 cells were incubated in an atmosphere with 5 % CO2 at 37 °C.
Healthy female 6–8 weeks Kunming mice (KM) weighted 15–20 g and healthy female ICR mice (18–20 g) were purchased from Fuzhou Wu's Animal Center.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo therapeutic effects of UCDs on H22 subcutaneous xenograft mouse model
ce:section.ce:para:
To establish the H22 subcutaneous xenograft models, the H22 cells with a concentration of 1 × 107 were inoculated subcutaneously into the healthy female ICR mice. One week after transplantation, the H22 subcutaneous tumor-bearing mice were divided into five groups at random and treated with PBS, UA NPs, SF, CDs, and UCDs, respectively. UA was administered at a dose of 15 mg/kg once every 2 days for 7 consecutive cycles. The body weights and tumor volume were supervised after every administration.
(1)
V = 1 / 2 ∗ L ∗
W 2
(Where V represents the tumor volume, L stands for the longest diameter of the tumor, while W refers to the shortest width of the tumor.)

After the mice were euthanized, the blood of the mice was gathered for biochemical detection. In addition, the main organs (Heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) and tumor tissue were gathered for hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining.

The H22 tumor-bearing mice tumor model was constructed by inoculating subcutaneously H22 cells into the female ICR mice. The H22 tumor-bearing mice were intravenously injected with CDs synthesized with our previously reported method [19], Cy5.5, or UCDs (15 mg/kg). At 24 h post-injection, fluorescence imaging was performed on mice using a PerkinElmer Caliper IVIS Lumina S5 imaging system. The mice were euthanized 24 h after injection, and the main organs of the mice were removed for in vivo imaging.

The construction of an orthotopic liver cancer model is as follows: Healthy female KM mice were placed on an injured operating table after routine anesthesia. After disinfection of the abdominal skin, a knife edge of about 1.5 cm was opened from the middle of the abdomen until the liver was exposed. The H22 cells taken from the abdomen of the mice were first lysed with red blood cell lysate and then washed with PBS twice, after that the cells were injected into mice at a density of 1 × 106 (50 μL) per mouse. The syringe pinhole was immediately solidified with gel to prevent tumor cells from leaking into the abdominal cavity. Finally, the wound suture was performed on the mice. After the end of the disinfection treatment with 75 % anhydrous ethanol, the mice were immediately heated and insulated and the status of the mice was carefully observed until the mice were awake.

On the fourth day after tumor inoculation in mice, the mice were divided into (a) PBS, (b) UA NPs, (c) SF, (d) CDs, and (e) UCDs groups, with 5 mice in each group, administered three times a week for 2 weeks and weighed every 2 days. All mice were sacrificed on day 40. The liver and tumor tissues were removed and weighed.
For the investigation of the survival of mice, the mice were in strict accordance with the above methods, with 5 mice in each group, and the death of mice was recorded at the end of the experiment.

Blood compatibility of UCDs was evaluated through a hemolysis test. In brief, mice blood was gathered in an anticoagulative EP tube. After being washed with PBS twice times by centrifugation (2500 rpm, 10 min), blood was fifty times diluted with PBS and divided into seven groups. Deionized water (positive control), PBS (negative control), and UCDs at five different concentrations (with the mixture concentration of 1 μg/mL, 5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, and 40 μg/mL) were added into blood tubes, respectively. Samples were standing for 3 h at 37 °C, the hemolysis study results were recorded by phone camera, and the absorbance at 540 nm was measured by a TECAN Infinite F200 Microplate Reader.

HepG2 cells were seeded in 12-well plates with a cell density of 105 cells per well for 24 h incubation. Subsequently, the cells were co-cultured with CD solution (100 μg/mL) for 0, 5, 10, 15, 30, and 60 min, respectively. Then, the cells were washed twice with PBS, and the cells were imaged using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, TCS SP8, Leica, Germany).

DCFH-DA was engaged to inspect intracellular ROS levels [20]. Unambiguously, the HepG2 cells were bred with varied concentrations of UCDs (25, 50, and 100 μg/mL at an equivalent concentration of CDs) in DMEM for 4 h. Thereupon, the cells were co-cultured with 0.2 μM DCFH-DA solution in a concentrator for an additional 20 min. Then washing, and imaging (Ex = 506 nm, Em = 531 nm) with CLSM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ellman’s test for intracellular GSH content
ce:section.ce:para: Intracellular GSH content was evaluated by the products colored in yellow of the reaction between DTNB and GSH in Ellman's analysis. HepG2 cells were incubated in a petri dish containing gradient concentrations of CDs (25–100 μg/mL) and UCDs for 12 h. Then, the absorbance of the mixture by mixing cell lysis and 50 μL DTNB at a concentration of 10 mM was measured by a microplate reader Infinite M200Pro to identify the product colored in yellow. Finally, the GSH content was detected by the standard curve of GSH.

Membrane integrity was studied by Dil staining [21]. Firstly, HepG2 cells were co-incubated with gradient concentrations of CDs (25–100 μg/mL) and UCDs for 24 h. After several times washing with PBS, 5 μM Dil was added for co-staining, incubated at 37 °C in the dark for 15 min, and images were collected at 549 nm (excitation) and 565 nm (emission) with CLSM.

For Mito-tracker analysis [22], the prepared CDs (25 μg/mL) and UCDs were co-incubated with HepG2 cells for 24 h, afterwards, the Mito-Tracker solution (200 nM) was added and co-incubated in a cell incubator for half an hour. The images were acquired with CLSM at an excitation of 488 nm and an emission of 528 nm (emission).

For transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis: The cells were co-incubated with Erastin, CDs (25 μg/mL), and UCDs for 24 h, and the cells were collected and fixed with Kano’s fixative at 4 °C for 8 h. Then, the cells were dehydrated with gradient ethanol and acetone solution, soaked and embedded, and then polymerized with epoxy resin. Then, the cells were sliced with a 0.5 μm slicer (Leica, Wetzlar, Germany), followed by washing with PBS several times, and the cell morphology was observed with HT7700 TEM at an accelerated voltage of 200 kV and analyzed by Gatan Microscopy Suite Software (Gatan, Las Vegas, NV, USA).

Female ICR mice were purchased from Fuzhou Wushi Animal Center. The Fuzhou University Animal Management and Use Committee (2021-SG-056) gave its approval to all animal procedures. The in vivo antitumor actions of UCDs and their counterparts were assessed on mouse xenograft models. Once the tumor volume reached 70 mm3, 1 × 105 H22 cells resuspended in 0.9 % NaCl solution were subcutaneously injected into ICR mice, the tumor-bearing mice were randomly grouped as (a) PBS; (b) UA NPs; (c) SF; (d) CDs; and (e) UCDs groups, respectively. For seven consecutive cycles, all mice received the medication every other day. In the meantime, the mice's body weight and tumor volume were monitored. Only the 0.9 % normal saline group was administered among them as a control. The mice were given the drug for two weeks before being anesthetizing and having their eyeballs removed so that blood could be collected. Major organs, serum, and tumor samples were gathered for a later study.

The whole blood cell analyzer was employed to detect the collected serum aspartate aminotransferase (AST), Alanine (ALT), Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN), and creatinine (Cre). The major organs, including the kidney, lung, liver, heart, and tumors, were fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde for 48 h before being dehydrated and embedded for subsequent H&E staining and immunohistochemistry (IHC) examination.

Data were revealed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Student’s t-test was employed for the analysis of statistical differences among different groups (*p < 0.05 represents for significant difference and **p < 0.01 for a very significant difference).

Inspired by the prosperous synthesis of natural product coffee-based nanozyme by Tu et al. 4, we discovered the CDs solution dialysis from the pentacyclic triterpenoid natural product UA powers emitted intense blue fluorescence upon 365 nm UV’s irradiation (Insert in Fig. 1
A). Moreover, the TEM images emerged that the UA-derived CDs were monodispersed with a diameter of an approximate grain particle size of 5.21 nm (Fig. 1 A), thus, the formation of CDs during hydrothermal treatment was preliminarily proved. Further, the explicit morphology of CDs was analyzed by atomic force microscopy (AFM), from which we noticed an average thickness of approximately 5–10 nm (Fig. 1 B and C). To dissect CDs in detail, we then inspected their photoluminescence (PL) essence by evaluating the FL emission spectrum upon varied excitation. As observed from Fig. 1 D and H, the three-dimensional fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs under excitation increases from 280 nm to 410 nm with an interval of 10 nm, and the emission wavelength is red-shifted from 350 nm to 520 nm, implying an excitation-dependent PL phenomenon, which may be ascribed to the combined effect of different sizes and multiple emission states [23]. Subsequently, we emphasized the structural composition of CDs. Firstly, the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra determined the C-H (-2923 cm−1), –OH (3417 cm−1), C = O (1670 cm−1), and C-O-C (1045 cm−1) stretching bands (Fig. 1 E), inferring the existence of hydroxy (–OH), carboxylic (–COOH), and carbonyl groups (C = O) in the prepared CDs [24,25]. Additionally, the XPS evaluation (Fig. 1 F and G) also confirmed the existence of two prominent peaks for C and O in CDs. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1 I) was decomposed into C-O bonds (286 eV) and C = O bonds (289 eV), which indicated the presence of C = O or C-O-C groups in CDs [5,26]. Furthermore, the 1H NMR spectra showed that the characteristic of methyl and hydrogen on the double bond of UA still exist after hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C, inferring the parent structure of UA was not destroyed. There was almost no obvious displacement of protons in the range of 0.5–5.5 ppm in the 1H NMR spectra of UA before and after hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 6 h ( Fig. S1 ), and its 1H NMR spectra almost coincided with CDs, confirming the excellent stability of UA. Furthermore, there is almost no significant change in the structural model of UA before and after hydrothermal reaction by analyzing the Mass spectrometry (MS) results, and the corresponding structural models were established ( Fig. S2 ), which demonstrated the same experimental results.

Currently, studies have confirmed the nanozyme activity towards the great majority of nanoparticles [27]. Hence, we next attempted to use Ellman’s test to explore the glutathione oxidase-like activity of CDs (Fig. 2
A). In a 5,5′-dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) assay, we found that CDs exhibited obvious GSH scavenging activity. Upon the addition of various GSH concentrations (0.5–4 mM) into 50 μg/mL of CDs in the presence of 100 μM DTNB, a time-dependent and GSH concentration-dependent absorbance at 422 nm was observed during the observed periods of 50 min (Fig. 2 B), verifying an apparent and effective clearance of GSH by CDs generated. In addition, the contents of GSH and oxidized glutathione (GSSG) were precisely determined by ultra-high liquid chromatography (HPLC), which verified the successful conversion of GSH to GSSG after CDs treatment ( Fig. S3 ).

To detect whether CDs consume oxygen in the catalytic process, ABTS was established as a probe to evaluate the generation of free radicals. Intriguingly, the oxidation of ABTS (ox-ABTS) by CDs in air atmosphere was time-dependent (Fig. 2 D). Compared with approximately 100 % oxygen atmosphere reaction environment, the catalytic process was significantly inhibited in the anaerobic nitrogen atmosphere ( Fig. S4 ), further verified that the CDs-mediated catalytic process required oxygen nutrition. In addition, the fluorescence intensity at 518 nm constantly increased with the increase of CDs concentration, which further verified that CDs had GSH oxidase-like activity. Based on the four-electron (4e−) reduction theory, we hypothesized that CDs may reduce oxygen to H2O2, hence extinguish –OH to aggrandize the catalytic process of GSH to GSSG [28–30]. Lastly, the potential of CDs was measured to be −4.3 ± 0.2 mV. Collectively, these results hinted that CDs possessed glutathione (GSH) oxidase-like catalytic activity (Fig. 2 C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the preparation of UCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Among the patterns of fabricating drug delivery systems, self-assembly plays a vital role in the region of both biomedicine and nanotechnology [31,32]. Herein, synergistic anti-hepatocellular carcinoma was prepared and optimized based on a green and simple self-assembly manner. It is interesting to note that the final size of self-assembled UCDs could be modulated ranging from nanometer to micrometer by adjusting the mass ratio of UA NPs to CDs from 20:1 to 1:20 ( Fig. S5 ). As shown in Fig. S6 , the Tyndall effect confirms that UCDs with uniform distribution can be synthesized by different mass ratios of UA NPs to CDs, once again verifying that UA is a self-assembled green template [11]. Additionally, the surface zeta potential of UCDs was −19.8 ± 1.2 mV when CDs (-1.3 ± 0.6 mV) were added to UA NPs (-15.2 ± 0.5 mV) (Fig. 3
L). This dipole–dipole interaction is mainly because a hydrogen atom is connected to an electronegative atom and a nearby electronegative atom by a pair of electrons, resulting in a hydrogen bond force. Interestingly, the nanostructures can stably stay even if the components of UA NPs and CDs are altered, which assumed that the outer layer of UA NPs was completely covered by CDs. Remarkably, both TEM (Fig. 3 A and C) and SEM (Fig. 3 B and D) morphology illustrated a thin spherical with consonant size distribution. Similarly, the size of UA NPs measured to be 184.7 ± 1.4 nm in size and PDI of 0.213 (Fig. 3 K). Thus, the screened UA NPs were selected for subsequent analysis. Similarly, by adjusting the mass ratio of UA NPs to CDs, the particle size of UCDs can fluctuate within a large range. In particular, the UCDs measured approximately 133.5 ± 1.2 nm in size and PDI of 0.246 with the optimal ratio of UA NPs to CDs (1:1, mass ratio), which could render a favorable EPR effect if these particles were administrated into the blood (Fig. 3 K). With such an optimal ratio, the surface charge of UCDs was determined to be −21.2 ± 0.8 mV. Particularly, negatively charged NPs were susceptible to reduced reticuloendothelial system (RES) or slower turbidity, facilitating the elongation of blood circulation time [33]. Therefore, the UCDs with such an optimal ratio of UA NPs to CDs (1:1, mass ratio) were selected for subsequent investigation. Also, these NPs displayed a good monodisperse spherical shape even in a dry environment, as observed in both TEM and SEM analysis (Fig. 3 A-D), demonstrating favorable colloidal stability of their co-assembled NPs. The stability of the colloid is immeasurably dependent on the ratio of UA NPs to CDs. When the ratio reaches more than 1:20, the particle size of the co-assembled measured by DLS increases obviously and ranges in 200–500 nm ( Fig. S1
A). By adjusting the mass ratio of UA NPs to CDs from 20:1 to 1:20, the surface charge of NPs changes from −17.4 ± 0.8 mV to −13.9 ± 3.1 mV ( Fig. S1
B). The finding demonstrates the potency of the hydrogen bonding between UA NPs and CDs. Furthermore, the electrical neutrality of UCDs at 1:20 led to considerable aggregation, and the size of the UCDs particle grew as the ratio of UA NPs to CDs increased from 1:1 to 1:20. Notably, excitation-dependent photoluminescence was seen in UCDs, where the excitation wavelength increases by 20 at intervals between 300 and 420 nm, the related emission wave increased first and subsequently dropped, which was ascribed to their defined size, the optical selection of particle surface deformities, and the influence of nitrogen band gap. Further evidence that CDs were successfully assembled into UA NPs came from the fluorescent signals found in UCDs (Fig. 3 E). However, compared to CDs alone, the fluorescence of UCDs was significantly diminished. This may be due to CDs stacking or exciton migration into UA NPs [19]. Further, the FTIR analysis (Fig. 3 F) exhibited obvious peaks at around 3449, 1627, 1353, and 1127 cm−1, which was ascribed to the stretch vibrations of –OH, C = C, C-O and C-O-C, respectively, inferring the existence of hydroxyl (–OH) and carboxyl (–COOH), which was consistent with the CDs and UA NPs reported in our previous study [17], implying the UCDs were indeed self-assembled by these two components, where they interacted with each other and were constructed into the UCDs at nanoscale.

A crucial metric for assessing the stability of self-assemblies is storage stability. Then, for up to 5 days, we conducted a storage study. Unexpectedly, neither the nanoparticles' particle size (Fig. 3 G) nor their zeta potential (Fig. 3 H) significantly changed. To put it another way, the particle sizes of UA NPs and UCDs (UA NPs/CDs = 1:1) grew to 278.3 ± 4.1 nm and 236.1 ± 2.4 nm, respectively, with a very modest increase over the original value. Additionally, the PDI value (Tables S1 and S2) confirmed the uniform size distribution of UA NPs and UCDs. Previous investigations have verified that TME has a weak acidic pH value. We modeled the construction and disassembly of UCDs under various pH conditions in vitro based on previous studies' confirmation that TME is slightly acidic [24,34]. Among these, pH 5.5 PBS was utilized to replicate the TME's acidic environment, whereas pH 7.4 PBS solution replicated the intravascular physical environment. At pH 5.5, the size distribution of UCDs widens with a PDI of 0.812, indicating the dissolution of certain NPs, according to the DLS determination (Fig. 3 I) and TEM morphologies ( Fig. S7 ). The protonation of amino groups in UA NPs in the TME was the main cause for the slight reduction of the potential of UCDs at pH 5.5 (Fig. 3 J) [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and antiproliferative effects of UCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the reassuring properties of UCDs, their expediency in fluorescence (FL) imaging and synergistic therapy were inspected at the cellular level. To analyze whether the UCDs can be uptake by living cells, we premeditated the carriage of UCDs across the HepG2 cell membrane by supervising the UCDs-mediated fluorescence intensity. The study implicated a rapid penetration of UCDs towards HepG2 cells within 10 min (Fig. 4
A), while the fluorescence intensity remained stable for at least 1 h, complying an exceptional biocompatibility and encouraging cellular uptake properties of CDs. The enviable cellular internalization of UCDs is attributed to the propensity of negative charges to be paired with a small number of positively charged ion-binding mechanisms on the cell membrane, which will further launch the comparatively slow “uptake and endocytosis” pathway of cellular internalization [19].

For in vitro antiproliferative activity, the cell viability of carcinoma cells (HepG2 and H22 cells) and normal cells (L02 cells) after being cultured with distinctive drugs was investigated by MTT assay. The cytotoxicity of UCDs and their counterparts were scrutinized in these three cell lines. As revealed in Fig. 4 E, from which we can notice that the L02 cells maintained a survival rate above 80 % despite the UCDs reaching a concentration of 20 μg/mL. On the contrary, as the drug concentration increases, the survival rate of H22 cells and HepG2 cells decreases rapidly (Fig. 4 C and D). By contrast, the cell viabilities of H22 and HepG2 cells dropped quickly with the concentration of UCDs increasing, namely 19.29 % of H22 cells and 21.74 % of HepG2 cells remained alive after treating with UCDs at 40 μg/mL, demonstrating higher cytotoxicity of the assemblies towards carcinoma cells than normal cells. Moreover, the CI computed＜1 (CI = 0.891) inbred a synergism in combining chemotherapy (UA NPs) and other therapeutic modalities (CDs).

For bioactive investigation of UA, cell viability of carcinoma cells (HepG2 cells) after being incubated with free UA, UA NPs, and CDs were investigated via MTT assay. The cytotoxicity of samples at various concentrations towards the HepG2 cells is displayed in Fig. S8 . From which one can see that the survival rate of UA and CDs was close. By contrast, cell viabilities of HepG2 cells treated with UA NPs decreased slightly faster with the concentration of UA increasing, namely, the survival rate of UA NPs, UA, and CDs were 32.6 %, 38.9 %, and 38.8 %, respectively, confirming the favorable stability of UA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cds activate ferroptosis through GSH consumption-dependent ROS generation
ce:section.ce:para:
Thereupon, we premeditated the GSH consumption activity of UCDs in HepG2 cells. As presented in Fig. S9 , the GSH level decreased virtually after CDs treatment, implying the oxidization of GSH into GSSG via glutathione oxidase. Since the depletion of GSH is accompanied by the activation of oxidative stress, DCFH-DA staining was engaged in this experiment to identify intracellular ROS generation. Conceivably, CDs displayed intense green fluorescence after being co-cultured with HepG2 cells (Fig. 5
C), apparently due to the adventure of a Fenton-like reaction to produce –OH. Afterward, we performed Western blotting experiments to identify the expression of ferroptosis markers (GPX4, NRF2, and COX-2). The study revealed that both CDs and UCDs significantly down-regulated the expression of GPX4 and NRF2 levels, while the expression of COX-2 was increased (Fig. 5 F), implying that both CDs and UCDs alternatively contributed to cell death through a ferroptosis-dependent fashion. Collectively, all data advocated that CDs and UCDs likely facilitated ferroptosis in HepG2 cells.

Given the significant character of mitochondria in ferroptosis, we further verified the relationship between ferroptosis and ultrastructural changes of mitochondria. As shown in Fig. 4 B, both CDs and UCDs lead to a dynamic adjustment from slender mitochondria to point-like structures, suggesting the occurrence of mitochondrial membrane concentration and volume reduction. Moreover, the Mitro-tracker staining illuminated both CDs and UCDs-induced alteration in mitochondrial morphology such as fragmentation and ridge distortion (Fig. 5 B). In addition, GSH consumption boosted cell membrane disturbance by advancing lipid peroxidation. Therefore, we utilized the lipophilic fluorescent probe Dil to monitor the integrity of the cell membrane. As shown in Fig. 5 A, both CDs and UCDs permanently caused decreased red fluorescence in HepG2 cells, implying the cell membrane structure has been damaged significantly with the increase of CDs concentrations, even more, there was almost no red fluorescence in the UCDs treatment groups. Hence, such outcomes financed that ferroptosis was actuated in HepG2 cells [36]. Collectively, these results proved that CDs induced ferroptosis under a GSH depletion-dependent ROS generation mechanism.

To further confirm the anti-cancer therapeutic activity of UCDs and their counterparts, we performed the Calcein-AM/PI double staining assays. As shown in Fig. 5 D, the distribution of living (Green) and dead (Red) HepG2 cells in the UA NPs, CDs, UCDs, and medium-only treated groups was consistent with the MTT findings. Surprisingly, most of the HepG2 cells in the UCDs-treated group were killed. Based on these encouraging outcomes, we then performed apoptosis examination by using Annexin V-FITC and PI co-staining ( Fig. S10 ) and the apoptosis rate of UA NPs, CDs, and UCDs determined were 37.53 %, 37.09 %, and 53.8 %, respectively. Collectively, the UCDs worked synergistically to eliminate tumors without recurrence.

Based on the aforementioned findings, we hypothesized that UCDs have the following anticancer mechanism (Fig. 5 E). UCDs significantly accumulated in the tumor tissue upon intravenous administration thanks to the EPR effect. When entering the TME with a lower pH, the disassembly happened as a result of the acid responses, and each component did its job as well as it could. On the one hand, CDs oxidize the GSH that is overexpressed into GSSG in TME, and the depletion of GSH causes the inactivation of glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4), causing lipid peroxidation to produce ROS accumulation and subsequently ferroptosis; on the other hand, UA NPs perform the chemotherapy function and ultimately achieve the efficacy of synergistic therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo therapeutic effects of UCDs against cancer in the H22 subcutaneous xenograft tumor model
ce:section.ce:para:
Inspired by these in vitro results, we further applied this synergistic therapy to mouse models of H22 subcutaneous xenograft tumors to verify the enhanced antitumor therapeutic effect. Firstly, the blood compatibility of the UCDs was evaluated by hemolysis assay to determine their safety in antitumor applications. As shown in Fig. S11 , in contrast to the positive control group, no obvious color of red blood cells was observed in different concentrations of UCDs, implying high hemocompatibility of the nano-assemblies. The IVIS spectrum imaging equipment was then used to measure the fluorescence distribution throughout the body. Mice with xenograft tumors were slaughtered 24 h after receiving injections of CDs made from our previously described, Cy5.5 and UCDs so that the primary organs and tumor tissues could be collected. As exhibited in Fig. 6
B, as compared with Cy5.5 and CDs, intense FL signal in the UCDs-treated group was detected from tumors until the end of observation (24 h), which authorized the laudable enrichment of the UCDs at tumor tissue. Worthily, both the liver and kidney also exhibited evident fluorescence signals, particularly at 24 h post-injection (Fig. 6 B and D), demonstrating that UCDs could be cleared from mice through the kidney and liver pathways. We consequently inspected the anticancer outcomes of CDs and UCDs on a xenograft nude mice model with H22 cells. For in vivo studies, ferroptosis inducer and FDA-approved first-line liver cancer drug SF were utilized as a positive control group [37].

Subsequently, the mice were randomly assigned to injection groups and given every other day for a total of 7 cycles of UA NPs (15 mg/kg), SF (15 mg/kg), CDs (15 mg/kg), and UCDs (Fig. 6 A). Tumor volume and body weight of mice were then recorded regularly the implement of five group treatments. Compared with the UA NPs, SF, and CDs treated groups, the tumor growth was greatly suppressed in the UCDs treatment group (Fig. 6 C and Fig. S12
A). As the medication was continued, no discernible weight change was seen ( Fig. S12
B), indicating the low toxicity of UCDs. Additionally, the average tumor weights of the PBS, UA NPs, SF, CDs, and UCDs treated groups were 258.0 ± 6.2, 217.2 ± 14.5, 179.1 ± 8.1, 144.3 ± 3.1, and 112.1 ± 9.1 mg, respectively (Fig. 6 E). Collectively, the above results confirmed the potential of UCDs in translational medicine for clinical anti-tumor unlimited potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosafety evaluation of UCDs and their counterparts
ce:section.ce:para: Given the biological toxicity of nanomaterials in the process of transformation from laboratory to clinical level, we analyzed the H&E staining histology in vivo. Histopathological and hematological tests displayed delicate nephrotoxic damage under SF handling, including glomerulosclerosis and elevated serum creatinine (Fig. 6 F-J), which was consistent with previous reported [29,31]. Compared with SF, UCDs and their counterparts were comparably safe for various organs including the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney. We also evaluated the biosafety of the applied treatments by examining the serum levels of Aspartate Aminotransferase (AST), Alanine (ALT), Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN) and Creatinine (Cre) from mice after treatment. The levels of these parameters in each group remained unchanged compared with the healthy samples, suggesting the inapparent acute toxicity from these treatments. Furthermore, the images of H&E staining in major organs (Heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) exhibited low to negligible injury in all groups (Fig. 6 F). Altogether, our findings demonstrate the excellent biosafety of UCDs and their counterparts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antitumor verification in the H22 orthotopic xenograft mouse model
ce:section.ce:para: We further investigated this “All in one” therapy on an orthotopic HCC tumor model to.

evaluate antitumor effects more realistically. To this end, mice were burdened with orthotopic HCC tumors from H22 cells with stable luciferase expression (H22-LUC). After orthotopic tumor inoculation for 3 days, these mouse models were divided into five groups, followed by intravenous injection with the treatment of PBS, UA NPs, SF, CDs, and UCDs (Fig. 7
B). During the treatment process, these H22-LUC orthotopic tumor-bearing mice models were under supervision by an in vivo imaging system. Non-invasive bioluminescence images of UCDs-treated mice showed a much lower bioluminescence signal level in the tumor region compared to those treated with PBS, UA NPs, SF, or CDs, implying the successful tumor inhibition with this “All in one” combinational therapy (Fig. 7 A). The mice in the UCDs treatment group also had the least liver weight in comparison to the PBS-treated group (Fig. 7 H), confirming the drug's maximum therapeutic efficacy. This anticancer efficacy was further corroborated by the bioluminescence intensity curve (Fig. 7 E), which demonstrated tumor growth inhibition. As shown in Fig. 7 C, we can intuitively perceive that UCDs injection group animals demonstrated the largest killing impact and inhibitory effect on liver cancer cells when compared to UA NPs, SF, and CDs treatment groups that only showed a minor inhibitory effect on liver cancer cells. The UCDs treatment had a tumor suppression rate (TSR%) of 79 %, which was significantly greater than the UA NPs (39 %), SF (45 %), and CDs (63 %) treatments, respectively (Fig. 7 G). As a result, all mice in the control group perished on day 12 while the median survival time of mice was greatly extended by the UCDs therapy and 80 % of mice were still living by day 40 (Fig. 7 D).

We noticed hardly any body weight changes in several groups throughout the investigation for biosafety (Fig. 7 F). Furthermore, no damage was discovered in the experimental treatment groups of UA NPs, SF, CDs, and UCDs when compared to the PBS injection group, which had obvious liver damage. We then performed histopathological analysis through H&E staining in vivo ( Fig. S13 ), and both histopathological and hematologic test results showed that mild nephrotoxic injury occurred mainly in the SF treatment group, which is consistent with previously reported results [38]. Besides, mice injected with CDs and UCDs were generally safe for several organs, such as the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney, when compared to animals treated with UA NPs and SF. Overall, UCDs demonstrated safe and effective anticancer activity in the orthotopic xenograft HCC mouse model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Turning “cold” into “hot” tumors through increasing immune cell infiltration
ce:section.ce:para:
We subsequently investigated the function of UCDs in controlling anti-tumor immune regulation since ferroptosis has great immunogenicity and promotes antigen presentation function [11], we further studied the immune cell infiltration in an immunologically “cold” tumor to “hot” further insight into the regulating effect of UCDs in the tumor immune microenvironment (TIME). As seen in Fig. 8
A, we found that CDs and UCDs both significantly aided CD4+ and CD8+ T cell infiltration in tumor tissues. On the contrary, UA NPs and SF did not show similar effects, but SF promoted the expression of CD56 and F4-80 in TME, which was consistent with previous reports [39]. Furthermore, the expression of CD56 and F4-80 was higher in CDs and UCDs compared to the SF therapy group, but more crucially, both CDs and UCDs dramatically increased the infiltration of NK cells and macrophages in tumor tissues (Fig. 8 B). Altogether, CDs and UCDs promoted the infiltration of T cells, NK cells, and macrophages, which resulted in the transformation of “cold” into “hot” tumors (Fig. 8 C). Even though UCDs have a wide range of potential applications in nanotechnology sectors like medication delivery systems and medical diagnosis [24,40]. Further research is necessary to determine whether UCDs can actually exert a variety of biological actions. Additionally, CDs biosafety has long been a prominent topic in research. Natural products with high biosafety, particularly their active components, have drawn a lot of interest as a source of bioactive CDs with low toxicity.

In conclusion, we described for the first time a novel nano-delivery system (UCDs) based on CDs-based enzymes made using UA as a precursor and a straightforward one-step hydrothermal process. A proof-of-concept verified anti-cancer CDs nanozyme that had good GSH oxidase-like activity. Our research also showed that natural product nanozymes have good medicinal value and are encouraged to advance ferroptosis to start the TME with little toxicity. Following the CDs' assembly with UA NPs, the co-assembled UCDs traveled to the site of action with a greater tumor concentration that was probably caused by the EPR effect. This allowed for a synergistic anticancer impact in vivo and eventually led to the tumor's complete eradication within one treatment cycle. Therefore, based on their preclinical investigations, these UCDs may be a good choice for the chemo-ferroptosis dual therapy against cancers. Importantly, no hazardous organic solvents were used in the preparation method to dissolve the hydrophobic precursors, allaying any safety worries (such as potential toxicity from carriers or organic solvents) that would hinder its application in clinics.
